mappings.key_insight: Rust eliminates null pointer exceptions at compile time. You must handle None explicitly.
Rust errors are values in the type system. The ? operator provides concise propagation without hiding error paths.
Rust has no garbage collector. Memory is freed deterministically when the owner goes out of scope.

mappings.rust: Immutable by default, let mut for mutable
mappings.key_insight: Rust makes immutability the default, requiring explicit opt-in to mutation with `mut`.
Rust prevents data races at compile time. The Send and Sync traits ensure safe sharing across threads.
Rust generics are resolved at compile time with zero runtime cost. Each concrete type gets optimized code.
Rust offers both static dispatch (generics) and dynamic dispatch (dyn Trait). Static dispatch is zero-cost.
Rust enums can hold different data per variant, making them as powerful as tagged unions with compiler exhaustiveness checks.
Rust match is exhaustive — the compiler ensures all cases are handled. It supports deep destructuring and guards.
Rust distinguishes owned vs borrowed strings. Both are always valid UTF-8. This prevents use-after-free and encoding errors.
Rust collections must be imported from std::collections (except Vec). They are generic and type-safe.
Rust closures have three trait categories based on how they capture: Fn (borrow), FnMut (mutable borrow), FnOnce (move).
Cargo handles building, testing, dependencies, and documentation in one tool. Crates.io is the package registry.
Rust testing is built into the language and toolchain. Unit tests live in the same file as the code they test.
Rust uses RAII — resources are automatically cleaned up when the owning variable goes out of scope. No explicit close/defer needed.
Rust iterators are lazy and zero-cost. Chain map/filter/fold operations that compile down to optimal loops.
Rust type aliases are transparent (no new type). Use the newtype pattern (struct Wrapper(Inner)) for distinct types.
Everything in Rust is private by default. Use pub, pub(crate), or pub(super) for fine-grained visibility control.
Rust async is zero-cost but requires choosing a runtime. Tokio is the most popular. Futures are lazy — they do nothing until polled.
Smart pointers provide different ownership strategies: Box (heap), Rc (shared), Arc (thread-safe shared), RefCell (interior mutability).
